author: Marc Elias
body:
I will not lower my voice5:03I abhor violence. Unlike the president, I have never celebrated the death of others nor incited a violent mob to ransack the Capitol. I have dedicated my life to fighting for free and fair elections through lawful means: organizing, protests and litigation.

In the last 10 years, Trump has attacked, demeaned and threatened nearly every prominent leader of the pro-democracy movement. He routinely attacks his perceived enemies but saves his harshest tactics for his political opponents.

Yet, time and time again, the legacy media insist on asking those of us who stand up to him to tone down our rhetoric. It is as if those of us who insist on the rule of law and call out Trump's authoritarianism are the problem. The suggestion is that perhaps, if we were just nicer to Trump, things in this country would be better.

Just yesterday, CNN’s Dana Bash asked Rep. Jamie Raskin if Democrats thought twice about their rhetoric against the president. The congressman quickly pointed out that any rhetoric against the president is directed at his authoritarian policies.

The media's insistence that Trump's critics are part of the problem gives cover to false equivalence — treating his lawlessness as one side of a legitimate debate.

In the run-up to the 2024 election, Trump posted on social media that if he won, lawyers, donors and election officials who had worked against him would be "prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law, which will include long-term prison sentences."

Refusing to back down, I responded that I would not be intimidated or allow him to suppress the vote, subvert the election or cheat.

Two days later, at the presidential debate in Philadelphia, ABC News' David Muir treated my response as a gotcha question for Kamala Harris: "One of your campaign's top lawyers responded, saying, 'We won't let Donald Trump intimidate us. We won't let him suppress the vote.' Is that what you believe he's trying to do here?"

Of course, it was what Trump was trying to do! What else could it possibly be other than an effort to intimidate and suppress the vote? Yet, by asking the question — in a debate nonetheless — Muir was subtly signaling there were two reasonable sides.

After winning the election, even Trump noted the change in how the big news outlets were treating him: "The media is tamed down a little bit," he observed. "They like us much better now, I think. If they don't, then we'll just have to take them on again, and we don't want to do that." He was not wrong.

While the legacy media may be “tamed down,” Trump has continued to take them on. Yet, he has found few signs of resistance.

The parent companies of ABC and CBS both agreed to pay him to settle bogus legal claims. The Washington Post altered its editorial stance, abandoning its decades-long practice of making presidential endorsements. The signs of media capitulation are nearly everywhere you look.

After taking office, Trump delivered a speech inside the Department of Justice calling me a "radical" and a "bad person" who, along with others, "tried to turn America into a corrupt, communist and Third World country."

He signed an executive order targeting my former law firm, punishing 1,200 lawyers for work I did years ago. Then, he went after my current law firm, attacking it and me by name and saying I was "grossly unethical."

Yet, I have yet to see the legacy media ask Trump about his attacks on me or others who have stood up to him. I have yet to read a single full-throated condemnation of Trump's dangerous rhetoric aimed at lawyers doing their job in protecting free and fair elections.

Even as Todd Blanche — Trump's acting Attorney General and former criminal defense lawyer — declares that investigating the president's political enemies is not abuse of power, the legacy media treats this as a debatable proposition.

They have certainly not lectured Blanche to watch his words or tone down his rhetoric.

The midterms are seven months away. Trump has signed executive orders to restrict voting. His Justice Department is trying to build a national database to disenfranchise voters. He has said he wants Republicans to “take over the voting.” His administration routinely claims there would be nothing wrong with deploying federal agents to the polls.

If calling this an affront to democracy and authoritarian overreach is too rhetorical for some in the legacy media, then perhaps it is they who need to rethink their words.

The answer is not for those who oppose this administration's lawlessness to go quietly. To the contrary, far too many people have stayed too silent for too long. The pro-democracy movement must be louder and bolder in its condemnation of the tyrannical actions unfolding around us.

I have been fighting Donald Trump in court and in public for 10 years. I am not stopping. And I will not be lowering my voice.
title: I will not lower my voice

author: Marc Elias
body:
The week an election denier faced the music5:30One of the first lessons I learned as a lawyer is that no one wants to have a legal problem. Hiring a lawyer, like going to the doctor, is born of necessity. Just as patients do not seek out interesting medical illnesses, clients do not celebrate fascinating legal problems.

When it comes to voting and election cases, too many lawyers and political observers forget this. The object is to enable voters to cast ballots under a fair set of rules and have those ballots counted. Lawyers may celebrate or pick apart the legal reasoning and the procedural developments, but the clients want to be able to vote.

I reflected on this as I read the analysis of a case my law firm won this week in Indiana. Last year, the state legislature removed college IDs from the accepted list of voter identification. On Tuesday, a federal judge agreed that our client was likely to prevail on the merits in his challenge to the law, and blocked it.

What happened next depends on where you stand.

Legal analysts point out that Indiana immediately appealed the ruling to the conservative 7th Circuit Court of Appeals. In their view, our victory remains incomplete and very much in doubt.

Yet, from the standpoint of our client — and many like him — it looks very different. On Wednesday morning, he voted using his college ID.

No matter what happens to the case from here, from his perspective, the case was a success. As a result of an Indiana law, he faced disenfranchisement. Because of our lawsuit, he instead cast his ballot.

The next time someone tries to sell you on a case as a victory or defeat, tell them you don't want to know what a law professor thinks — you want to know whether the client believes they prevailed.

That is how I judge my court wins and losses. And on that score, Indiana was a complete success.

## What worried me most this week

Thursday brought bad news from North Carolina. The state’s Republican-controlled board of elections announced it is [sharing](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/north-carolina-election-boards-gop-majority-approves-sharing-voter-data-with-dhs/) its unredacted voter data with the Trump administration.

North Carolina claims it will use the Department of Homeland Security’s SAVE database to check voter rolls. The immigration database is decades old, and notoriously inaccurate. It has been known to erroneously flag U.S. citizens as noncitizens.

This is both a safety concern and a voter suppression tool. The Trump administration has proven to be careless when it comes to checking for immigration status. The use of this system in North Carolina will surely put U.S. citizens in harm's way.

It will also delay eligible voters who were inaccurately flagged. Voters may not be aware that their registration has been affected until they show up at the polls. If they are informed, it may be too late to re-register.

This is just the latest example of the GOP-controlled North Carolina election board taking steps to disenfranchise voters. Though I am worried, North Carolina should know I am also watching.

## What I'm watching in the courts

Louisiana is [suing](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/louisiana-sues-key-federal-election-agency-to-implement-its-anti-voting-law/) the U.S. Election Assistance Commission (EAC) for blocking part of the state’s restrictive proof of citizenship law. Louisiana passed its anti-voting law in 2024, but the EAC split on whether to approve adding new state-specific language requiring citizenship documents to register to the federal voter registration form. Without a majority, the request was denied.

Louisiana isn’t letting it go. While Louisiana claims the EAC is violating the National Voter Registration Act (NVRA), its motivation is much simpler: The Republican-controlled state is trying to disenfranchise voters.

As I have pointed out before, proving citizenship is not easy. Many people don’t have passports — and if the document is expired, it’s not simple or inexpensive to get a new one. Plenty of people don’t have easy access to their original birth certificate. And the problems facing women who change their legal names when they are married are well-documented.

Republicans in Louisiana aren’t blind to these hurdles. In fact, they are taking advantage of them. That’s why my firm was already fighting hard to protect the voters in Louisiana who would be affected by this law. Keep an eye on this new case — it’s an important one.

## What gave me hope this week

John Eastman has been [disbarred](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/key-trump-lawyer-who-tried-to-overturn-2020-election-permanently-disbarred/).

Eastman was a key architect in the failed attempt to overturn the 2020 election. The former Trump lawyer wrote a series of memos outlining ways to keep Trump in power after the election — including the infamous debunked legal theory suggesting that then-Vice President Mike Pence could reject electoral votes from key battleground states.

Pence rejected the plan, and it ultimately culminated in the Jan. 6 attack on the Capitol.

Shortly after Jan. 6, 2021, I wrote about the need for accountability for the lawyers involved in undermining the 2020 election. At long last, the California Supreme Court agreed — declining to intervene after lower courts found Eastman advanced baseless claims to help Trump cling to power.

Other election deniers and sycophants serving Trump should take note. There are consequences for their actions, and Trump will not save them in the end. I have hope that others — from the last administration to this one — will be held accountable for the damage they have done to our democracy.
title: The week an election denier faced the music

author: Marc Elias
body:
Ron DeSantis is stomping on voting rights — one boot at a time4:39Donald Trump’s SAVE America Act is dead. After weeks of lies and threats, Senate Republicans now admit they cannot pass it. Yet, that does not mean the threat is behind us.

On Wednesday, Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis — a man Trump once called “Meatball Ron” — [signed](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/desantis-attacks-elections-signing-florida-version-of-save-act-into-law/) his own state version of the anti-voting legislation into law. Now, starting in 2027, Floridians will have to prove their citizenship status in order to register to vote. Student ID will no longer be an acceptable form of voter identification.

That is, unless my law firm’s lawsuit stops it first. For weeks, I had publicly promised we would sue to block the new law. To time the lawsuit with the governor’s signature, I had the misfortune of watching DeSantis’ awkward bill signing via livestream.

Trying his best to seem normal, he took voting rights away from his own residents with one stroke of his pen.

This isn’t the first time DeSantis has made it [harder](https://www.democracydocket.com/analysis/florida-gov-ron-desantis-record-on-voting-rights/) to vote in the Sunshine State. Nor the first time I have sued. In fact, DeSantis and I have been sparring over his war on voting for years.

In May 2020, I [sued](https://www.democracydocket.com/cases/florida-covid-19-election-relief/) DeSantis over his failure of a voting plan during the COVID pandemic. By July, we had exacted a settlement to help mitigate the challenges voters would face.

In 2021, DeSantis and GOP lawmakers famously banned anyone except poll workers from distributing food and water to voters waiting in line, cut the period over which absentee ballot requests will be honored and shortened the time drop boxes may be used. I sued him six minutes after he signed the law.

Then, in 2023, Florida targeted voter registration groups with a host of new restrictions. Once again, we [sued](https://www.democracydocket.com/cases/florida-omnibus-voter-suppression-law-challenge/) and won a partial victory that the state of Florida is still appealing.

Over the years, I have sued Florida over its targeting of college students, restricting mail-in voting and, on several occasions, redistricting. The latter of which DeSantis has been particularly brazen.

Despite a ban on partisan gerrymandering in the state constitution, DeSantis has repeatedly insisted on maps that disadvantage Democrats and their candidates. He has also disregarded a provision that is intended to protect minorities in the redistricting process.

DeSantis also created an office dedicated to investigating and prosecuting election crimes and allegations of illegal voting — of which there are virtually none. Yet, on Tuesday, he bragged, “We’ve now brought more prosecutions for voter fraud in Florida since that office was created than probably everywhere else in the country combined at this point.”

Of course, that is not the full story:

Many of the people prosecuted are formerly incarcerated individuals who were told they were able to vote, after Floridians overwhelmingly passed a ballot measure restoring their rights in 2018.

The GOP legislature then responded by weakening the measure — now, if you have a criminal record in Florida, your voting rights aren’t restored until you pay all fines and fees.

After doing so, the Florida government took this opportunity to target its own residents. In 2022, Florida officials loudly [announced](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/florida-governor-announces-20-voter-fraud-charges-in-press-conference-touting-new-election-crimes-office/) that they charged 20 people with alleged voter fraud for voting in the 2020 election. While many of the cases were dismissed in the end, it doesn’t take away the suffering these people endured. They didn’t know they were breaking the law — they were just trying to be part of their community.

As the midterms get closer and DeSantis is eyeing a 2028 presidential run, his policies and rhetoric are growing more and more extreme. While he has always been a proud vote suppressor, he was not always an election denier. In 2023, he dismissed Trump’s fraud claims, saying, “all those theories that were put out did not prove to be true.”

Now, when it comes to the 2020 election, he is starting to sound a lot like his former opponent.

“We saw what happened in the 2020 election, in some of these states where they had the mass mail balloting. You had ballot harvesting, Zuckerbucks,” the governor said on Tuesday. “You had all these different things [that] were happening where no one knew what the hell was going on.”

While those in his audience at the recent bill signing may not know what is going on, DeSantis certainly does. He is a Harvard-trained lawyer. But he is also a political opportunist. Ron DeSantis, with his stupid little lifted boots, is attacking voting for political gain. I’m glad he feels small. He should.
title: Ron DeSantis is stomping on voting rights — one boot at a time

author: Marc Elias
body:
There are no two sides to a confession5:54In my darkest moments, I fear that this is how democracy dies. Not with a secret conspiracy conducted in the middle of the night. But with a brazen confession made public in the light of day.

Last night, after a long day, I sat down to reflect on *Watson v. RNC* — the Supreme Court case that could decide the results of the midterm elections. I have poured years into the case and others like it, trying to ensure voters are not disenfranchised by delays caused by the U.S. postal system.

As counsel to the private party defendants, I had hoped to argue the case, but the conservative Supreme Court had other plans. Prompted by the state of Mississippi, the Court denied my option to have time to argue.

So, as the RNC presented its case for why mail ballots should not be counted if received after Election Day — even if postmarked in time — all I could do was listen.

After rereading the transcript, I turned to the media’s coverage of the day. Some thought we would win, others predicted a loss. All, however, treated the RNC’s position as normal and legally reasonable.

I have been doing this work for over 30 years. From my early days as a young lawyer looking to make a difference to running my own pro-democracy law firm, I have dedicated my career to free and fair elections.

Over the years, I have watched the Republican Party morph into a crime family that boasts about rigging elections. They have told us, in courtrooms and legislative chambers and at campaign rallies, exactly what they are doing and exactly why.

They want to limit the right to vote of minorities and others who oppose them. They seek to use technology to gerrymander themselves into permanent majorities. And, if all else fails, they want election deniers in a position to seize ballots or refuse to certify results.

Despite the proof before their eyes, too many are unable to accept the confession.

For example, in their complaint in *Watson*, the RNC cited data showing that Democratic voters disproportionately utilize vote by mail and mail their ballots late. Then they went a step further. In a federal court filing, the RNC had the audacity to write that counting these ballots "specifically and disproportionately harms Republican candidates and voters.".

In 2013, well before Donald Trump became the dominant force in the GOP, North Carolina Republicans were enacting laws to disenfranchise Black voters "with near surgical precision." The federal court of appeals found that the party had requested data on which voting methods were disproportionately used by Black voters and then eliminated them. The GOP learned from it, refined its methods and kept going.

By 2021, Republican lawyers felt secure enough to say the quiet part out loud before the Supreme Court. In *Brnovich v. Democratic National Committee*, Justice Amy Coney Barrett asked the Republican lawyer why the GOP had an interest in keeping restrictive Arizona voting laws on the books. He did not lean on false claims of fraud or election integrity. He said bluntly, "Because it puts us at a competitive disadvantage relative to Democrats. Politics is a zero-sum game."

That statement should have ended the debate about Republican motives once and for all. Instead, it was noted, contextualized and largely forgotten.

Even when Republicans admit exactly what they are doing, too many people who know better find a way to excuse it.

In 2016, a North Carolina Republican legislator announced his party would draw congressional maps giving Republicans a 10-3 advantage because, as he put it, "electing Republicans is better than electing Democrats." He added, with breathtaking candor, that he would have preferred an 11-2 map but didn't think it was geometrically possible.

When Trump pressured Texas to redraw its congressional maps mid-decade — explicitly to gain five additional Republican House seats — the media could not pretend the motive was unclear. In allowing the map to take effect, Justice Alito declared it "indisputable" that Texas had acted for "partisan advantage pure and simple."

Many in the media and chattering class responded by treating both parties as equally guilty — never mind that one party has spent decades in court fighting to remove partisan considerations from the process while the other has resisted even incremental reforms.

Our country has spent decades treating politics as a game between two teams, each with legitimate grievances. That framework may have made sense when both parties operated within the democratic rules of the road. But that hasn't been true for more than a decade — and it is getting worse.

Trump has now made the SAVE America Act his top legislative priority, threatening to refuse to sign any legislation until it passes. His explanation for why Republicans need it was, as usual, unambiguous. "We'll never lose a race. For 50 years, we won't lose a race."

Rather than examining why the GOP is investing such enormous political capital in a bill that strips voting rights from millions of Americans, the media focuses on whether it might backfire politically.

I am writing this because I want to warn you and because I want there to be a record.

The Republican Party is telling us, with brimming confidence and zero shame, that it intends to make it harder for its opponents to vote and easier to control the outcome of elections. They are prepared to use every tool available to them — legal or not — to stay in power.

There are no two sides to a confession. There is only the confession — and the choice of whether to take it seriously.

I have given up on legacy media taking it seriously. My question is: Will you?
title: There are no two sides to a confession

author: Marc Elias
body:
First Fulton, now Maricopa: Trump's plan to subvert the 2026 elections4:51Donald Trump told us he wants Republicans to "take over the voting in at least 15 places" — and that he "should have" seized ballots and voting equipment after the 2020 election. Now, he is making good on those threats.

Less than two months after the FBI [raided](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/justice-department-fulton-county-georgia-search-warrant-affidavit/) Fulton County's Election Center, Trump's Department of Justice has [turned on](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/trumpfbi-subpoena-2020-records-maricopa-county-arizona/) a second target: Maricopa County. The move follows a familiar script.

Yesterday, Warren Petersen — the GOP president of the state Senate — announced that he had complied with an FBI subpoena, turning over records related to the 2020 election. "The FBI has the records," he announced ominously on social media. Only hours earlier, Trump had shared news of the seizure on Truth Social, calling it "Great!!!"

Trump didn't choose to target Maricopa County at random. Arizona's largest county is at the center of 2020 election conspiracies. False claims of fraud led to a nonsensical legislative audit.

Secretary of State Adrian Fontes described it on [my podcast](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=b0rCb--xaNA) as a self-sustaining "grift, fueled by conspiracy theories." That audit has been public record for years, making the FBI's subpoena not just unnecessary, but telling.

There is absolutely no legal reason for the FBI to subpoena these records. But that is not the concern of Trump or his Department of Justice. They have only one goal for this seizure: to help Donald Trump.

Those of you who follow my writing know I warned that Trump was raising the stakes, that Republicans were willing to help, and that he was telegraphing a playbook to steal the 2026 elections.

That moment has arrived. Trump is no longer telling us his plan; he is enacting it.

Trump wanted to take over voting in at least 15 places. He previously said it might include Detroit and Philadelphia. We now know to add Atlanta and Phoenix to that list.

In Fulton County, the FBI raided the election center. Yes, they want to continue the Big Lie from 2020, but equally important, it was a dry run for 2026.

That raid handed Trump thousands of election records. With those documents in hand, the false claims of fraud will grow louder. A fake justification for federal authority over elections will be conjured out of thin air.

In Arizona, the story is similar. The FBI's subpoena for Maricopa County election records is intended to signal that a criminal investigation of the 2020 results is ongoing.

The goal, once again, is to gain knowledge for future actions while sowing doubt about the integrity of our elections. As in Georgia, Trump will use this investigation to demand further power over Arizona's elections, just in time for 2026.

And this is only the beginning. At least 13 more states are on Trump's list. The most obvious targets are those that delivered the election to Joe Biden — Michigan, Pennsylvania, Nevada and Wisconsin — but the reach extends further.

In blue states, cities like New York and Chicago are vulnerable. In Republican-controlled states, cities like Houston, Jacksonville and Columbus — where urban voters can decide statewide outcomes — are equally at risk.

Trump's war on the 2026 elections is only getting started.

He has already issued executive orders that have been blocked. His DOJ's efforts to secure access to sensitive voter data have been mired in court defeats. Now, he is threatening congressional leaders if they don't enact his mass voter-suppression law.

But if those efforts fail, he will want the option to simply seize the ballots and declare the winners. That is why what is happening in Fulton County and Maricopa County is so important. These actions are not just about relitigating the past — they are about subverting the future.

The good news is that there is still time to stop this assault on our democracy, and we know how to do it.

Elected officials must stand firm against voter-suppression laws. Blue states must harden their defenses. Democratic campaigns and committees must treat these threats with the same urgency as any advertising or turnout battle. The legal community must be ready to fight on multiple fronts at once. And the media must stop treating this as ordinary partisan politics — it is an authoritarian power grab, and it should be covered as such.

When our democracy hangs by a thread, and our elections are on the line, we cannot afford to operate as if the same rulebook still applies. The FBI and DOJ are no longer independent institutions; they operate to advance Trump's partisan agenda.

I am preparing for the fight ahead. Trump has built an army and weaponized the justice system to secure his power. We are not too late — but the time to stop him is now.
title: First Fulton, now Maricopa: Trump's plan to subvert the 2026 elections

author: Marc Elias
body:
The week Trump came for your vote and your news4:39This was a hard week for democracy.

On Tuesday night, Donald Trump [lied](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ecmky5jtYiI) his way through the State of the Union, ordering Senate Majority Leader John Thune to go beyond the already disastrous SAVE America Act by requiring proof of citizenship to vote and banning no-excuse mail-in voting.

On Friday, I told you this would be a calamity for our elections and warned that Trump wasn't asking Congress to make these changes — he was demanding them. Now, Trump is taking matters into his own hands.

According to reports, [anti-voting activists](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/white-house-circulating-blatantly-illegal-draft-emergency-order-to-take-control-of-elections/) are working with the White House to issue an emergency executive order that would give Trump unprecedented control over our elections. The order is based on a false conspiracy theory that China interfered in the 2020 election.

Yes, it is illegal and unconstitutional, and yes, the courts would block it. But that does not mean the mere issuance of such an [order](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/read-the-laughable-legal-memo-behind-the-claim-that-trump-can-declare-a-national-voting-emergency/) would be harmless. It would cause real damage.

Trump is ready and willing to take drastic measures to swing the midterms in his favor. Every day that passes, his approval ratings sink further. His policies are growing more unpopular. The calls to release the Epstein files are growing louder.

On top of this, the DOJ is filing [five new lawsuits](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/trump-doj-expands-voter-roll-crusade-sues-five-more-states/) to obtain access to sensitive voter data. The new lawsuits — targeting Utah, Oklahoma, Kentucky, West Virginia, and New Jersey — bring the total number of states sued to 30.

Speaker Mike Johnson said it himself: "If we lose the midterms, heaven forbid, it would be the end of the Trump presidency in a real effect."

## What worried me most this week

Bari Weiss and David Ellison just took control of CNN.

After months of wavering between Netflix and Paramount Skydance, the Warner Bros. board has agreed to sell the company to Paramount — and with it, CNN.

Ellison has already installed Bari Weiss at the helm of CBS News, where she quickly transformed the Tiffany Network into the Trump Network. I expect CNN is heading toward a similar fate.

In countries like Russia and North Korea, authoritarian leaders control the media, corrupting news outlets to spread propaganda and serve their political interests. During Trump's second term, American media companies have capitulated in much the same way. With CNN now under Ellison's control, it will only get worse.

The only question remaining is whether independent, pro-democracy news outlets like Democracy Docket can scale fast enough to fill the void. If not, there will be shockingly little trustworthy news left for those who don't want a steady drumbeat of pro-Trump propaganda.

Thanks to members like you, Democracy Docket has a strong base of support on which to build. If you can afford to contribute a little extra, [now is a great time to do it](https://share.hsforms.com/2wvnpNW8_St-bbe33uxf1VQspgbo). You will help keep Democracy Docket's website and daily and weekly newsletters free for everyone.

## What I'm watching in the courts

In Fulton County, two former DOJ officials submitted a court filing [accusing](https://www.democracydocket.com/news-alerts/ex-justice-department-officials-fbi-fulton-county-2020-elections-raid/) the department of failing "to live up to the high standard set by previous administrations" in its seizure of 2020 election records. The officials — former acting U.S. attorneys general Peter Keisler and Stuart Gerson — went further, condemning the department for abandoning its role as "a protector of constitutional rights and liberties."

However, ahead of an upcoming evidentiary hearing, federal Judge Jean-Paul Boulee reversed course, directing the DOJ and Fulton County to reach an agreement about the seized records outside of court before he considers additional evidence. Boulee also suggested that the DOJ may be permitted to keep the ballots it seized, regardless of the outcome in the case.

With the 2026 midterms right around the corner, that would be a terrible outcome — one that would allow Pam Bondi's DOJ to seize election records without fear of consequence.

## What gave me hope this week

Following recent wins in redistricting, Democrats are poised to take the House in November. While Republicans launched the redistricting fight in Texas, they have lost the war. At best, they will break even. At worst, they will actually lose seats.

And while the Supreme Court may eventually gut what remains of the Voting Rights Act in Louisiana v. Callais, it looks increasingly unlikely it will do so in time to affect the 2026 elections.

Still, we cannot afford to rest easy. We must continue to fight back against attacks on our democracy — but I believe we are poised to win.
title: The week Trump came for your vote and your news

author: Marc Elias
body:
In Trump's politics, there is no quiet part4:49If there is one phrase I would like to see retired from analysis of the Trump administration, it is that they have “said the quiet part out loud.” The phrase, which originated in an episode of *The Simpsons* in the mid-1990s, is meant to suggest that someone accidentally says something they intended to keep secret.

Here is the not-so-secret truth: In Donald Trump’s politics, there is no quiet part. Everything is intended to be said aloud for everyone to hear.

If Donald Trump wants a plane from a foreign government, he asks out loud. If he wants $10 billion from the U.S. Treasury, he simply sues the IRS and leaves it to his own government loyalists to sort out the details. If he wants awards in his honor, he demands them.

In recent weeks, he has started expressing his desires for the 2026 midterm elections out loud. If Trump were to have it his way, we wouldn’t “even have an election.” Since that is not an option, his preferred second choice is for Republicans to “take over the voting in at least 15 places.”

While he hasn’t identified the full list of places where Republicans would control voting, he has singled out Atlanta, Detroit and Philadelphia — the first two of which have key Senate races this fall — as places he believes have corrupt elections.

Looking out for his razor-thin House majority, Speaker Mike Johnson added California to the list. While acknowledging that he lacked any proof for his claim, Johnson suggested that Republicans might have lost three House seats in California due to fraud.

Most recently, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem boasted that “When it gets to Election Day,” her agency has “been proactive to make sure we have the right people voting, electing the right leaders to lead this country.” Noem’s performance and ethics may be under attack, but she knows how to please the boss.

Her statement has three important components. First, she makes clear that she will use the power of the government “proactively.” Second, she will make sure the “right people” are able to vote. Most importantly, she has her eye on the ultimate prize: “electing the right leaders.” She understands that, for Trump, there is no point in having the right people voting if they elect the wrong candidates.

It is not just government officials who are willing to say out loud how they plan to secure a Republican victory. Steve Bannon recently announced that “We’re going to have ICE surround the polls come November.”

When asked about this, professional White House propagandist Karoline Leavitt said that she “can’t guarantee” that ICE agents wouldn’t be near voting locations. In a Senate hearing, the acting head of ICE, Todd Lyons, acknowledged that there would be “no reason” for ICE to be near polling places during voting. However, when asked whether he would disobey an order from Noem to deploy agents to voting locations, he stammered and did not answer directly.

That actually made Lyons unusual. He was not willing to say out loud what is nearly certain to be true: If Trump wants ICE to surround the polls — as Bannon suggests — ICE will surround the polls, and no one in a position to object will do so. I suspect that may end his career with this administration. If you’re not willing to say it out loud, what use does Trump have for you?

It was once taken for granted that the political misuse of government would be done in secret. Nixon did not broadcast the Watergate break-in. Reagan did not advertise the Iran-Contra affair.

This led to a widespread belief in Washington, D.C., that the cover-up is always worse than the crime. Indeed, it was the cover-up that often signaled to the media and the public that there was a serious crime in the first place.

But what if the crime is stealing an election? And what if there is no cover-up because they celebrate what they are doing?

Those of us who care about free and fair elections need to recognize that, in the Trump era, secrecy is not essential to rigging an election. Democracy can die in the plain light of day as easily as it does in darkness. It can be undermined by members of Congress as readily as by mobs storming the Capitol. Cabinet secretaries can be more dangerous than local vote-suppressing vigilantes.

If we’re too focused on listening for whispers from the White House, we will close our ears to what Trump is saying out loud. We must not war-game improbable scenarios when his administration is giving us a playbook of its next moves.

To paraphrase George Orwell, we must not reject the evidence of our own eyes and ears. If we are to survive this period with democracy intact, that is the most essential command.
title: In Trump’s politics, there is no quiet part

author: Marc Elias
body:
Trump has never accepted election results and he is only getting worse2:35Let’s be honest. He is getting worse.

In the last 24 hours, Donald Trump has stood before an audience of religious leaders and declared that the 2024 election was rigged. He sat down for an interview with NBC News and claimed that China is involved in our elections. He went on Truth Social and spread right-wing conspiracy theories.

While Trump has been banging the rigged-election drum for years, it is getting worse.

In 2016, Trump said he would “totally accept” the election results “if he wins.” During a debate, when asked if he would concede the election, he said, “I will keep you in suspense.” Yet election denialism was not yet central to his political movement.

At the time, people were eager to live by a now-discredited rule: Take Trump seriously, but not literally. His threats to dismiss the election results were taken as a joke. People doubted he would win, and they definitely doubted he had the movement and influence to act on any such threats.

Even when Trump won, his threats to democracy did not stop. In 2017, he established a presidential commission to look into fraud in the election he won. While it ultimately collapsed under its own incompetence, it should have been a warning.

By the 2020 election, Trump had ramped up a campaign of election denialism and voter suppression to a fever pitch. In July 2020, when Fox News asked if he would accept the election results, he said, “Look … I have to see. No, I’m not going to just say yes. I’m not going to say no, and I didn’t last time either.”

When he lost the election, his answer became a firm “no.” Frivolous lawsuits, abuse of election officials, and violence ensued.

Unrepentant, he was back at it in 2024 — this time hinting at the violence he unleashed four years earlier. “If everything’s honest, I’ll gladly accept the results. I don’t change on that. If it’s not, you have to fight for the right of the country.”

Before Trump, the peaceful transfer of power was not even a question. The wheels of democracy turned every four years, no matter who won or who lost. Former presidents attended inaugurations. The baton was passed from one president to another. They were graceful in defeat and humble in victory.

Sadly, that is no longer true.

As I said, the last 24 hours have been filled with an endless heap of election conspiracy. Last night, during an interview with NBC News, he said, “There are some areas in our country that are extremely corrupt. They have very corrupt elections… If there was cheating, which there was, but if there was cheating, it should be found because we can’t let it happen again.”

Then, during the National Prayer Breakfast, he said, “They rigged the second election. I had to win it. I needed it for my own ego. I would’ve had a bad ego for the rest of my life. Now I really have a big ego. Beating these lunatics was incredible... The first time they said I didn’t win the popular vote. I did.”

This is probably the closest Trump has come to identifying what is really going on. Yes, he is an authoritarian, but he is also a narcissist. He is driven by an uncontrolled ego.

While other presidents have sought to bring the best and the brightest into government, Trump surrounds himself with incompetent sycophants and blind loyalists.

In past elections, this meant refusing to admit the reality of election defeats and spinning lies even when he won. In 2021, it also led to violence.

But this year, it is taking on a different cast entirely.

In 2020, he attacked the electoral system from the outside. Even though he was president, his destructive actions were taken in his role as a candidate. The White House staff and the Department of Justice prevented him from using the military to seize ballots. They refused to engage in litigation against state certifications.

That has all changed. Now, the DOJ acts as Trump’s personal election-denying law firm. The White House counsel seems to exist to facilitate whatever outrageous action Trump wants to take. There are no internal guardrails or truth-tellers.

As Trump threatens this year’s elections, he operates against the cold reality that he has never accepted an election he lost. He won’t accept it when he loses control of Congress this fall. He will try to overturn it however he can, with the full range of tools available to him as president.

Those of us in the pro-democracy movement have our own tools. Now is the time to sort and sharpen them and get ready to use them. It’s going to be a busy year.
title: Trump has never accepted election results and he is only getting worse

author: Marc Elias
body:
To restore the rule of law, Bondi wants us to hand over democracy4:49On Saturday morning, Alex Pretti was killed by federal agents in Minneapolis. While trying to assist a woman who had been pepper-sprayed, he was grabbed and beaten by at least six officers. He was shot 10 times by at least two of them.

Within minutes, administration officials were defaming the dead man. They called him a domestic terrorist and a would-be assassin. Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino even claimed that Pretti “wanted to do maximum damage and massacre law enforcement.”

Following the killing of Renée Good, all of this felt disturbingly familiar: the shooting of a U.S. citizen who was observing and recording federal agents; the lies and smears from the White House and its allies; the obstruction of any investigation; and the grieving family and community left behind.

But this time there was a sick twist. Later that day, U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi sent a letter to Minnesota’s Democratic governor laying out her demands to “restore the rule of law.” The first two were the usual Republican fare: Allow the federal government to investigate fraud and cooperate more closely with ICE.

The third request, however, was something entirely different: “Allow the Civil Rights Division of the Department of Justice to access voter rolls to confirm that Minnesota’s voter registration practices comply with federal law as authorized by the Civil Rights Act of 1960.” This, she wrote, would “better guarantee free and fair elections and boost confidence in the rule of law.”

In short, in the wake of a tragic killing, Bondi sought to leverage the state’s need for calm into turning over sensitive voting data on millions of citizens. While this might seem outrageous, it was not random.

Donald Trump is obsessed with the midterms and will do whatever he needs to prevent losing control of Congress. Given his unpopularity, that likely means mass voter suppression, election subversion and outright election theft.

To accomplish any of this at scale, he will need unredacted statewide voter files. That would allow him and Bondi to identify which voters to target, which ballots to discard, and against whom to lodge false claims of election fraud.

The Department of Justice has already demanded this information from all 50 states and the District of Columbia. While several red states have voluntarily complied, nearly all blue and purple states have resisted.

As a result, the DOJ has filed lawsuits against 23 states and Washington, D.C. I know these cases well because my law firm is defending voters from this intrusion on their privacy in every single one of them.

So far, the DOJ has lost the first three cases in California, Georgia and Oregon. With these failures weighing on her, I have no doubt that Bondi saw the tragedy in Minnesota as an opportunity to gain access to these records.

It might seem random that Trump is spending so much time and resources targeting Minnesota, but it actually makes sense. For Trump, it’s personal.

All three times he ran for president, he lost the state. Each time, he falsely claimed the loss was the result of fraud. Minnesota is bordered by Trump strongholds like North Dakota, South Dakota and Iowa. Its only other neighbor is Wisconsin, which twice voted for Trump.

Then there are Minnesota’s demographics. It is overwhelmingly white and Christian. In Trump’s racist narrative, that alone is enough “proof” of election fraud.

If Trump and his DOJ gained access to Minnesota’s voter file now, it could supercharge this false narrative. He and his cronies could game out disenfranchisement schemes well in advance of the midterms. Most of all, it would hand them a much-needed win at a time when they are floundering in court.

Donald Trump doesn’t care about law enforcement. If he did, he never would have pardoned those who assaulted police officers on Jan. 6. As his more recent pardons make clear, he doesn’t care about fraud or government waste either.

Donald Trump cares about one thing: Donald Trump. And those around him know it.

That is why Pam Bondi demanded voter records in the wake of a tragedy. That is why she is weaponizing the criminal justice system against Trump’s opponents. And that is why, when the time comes, she will use the power of the Department of Justice to interfere with free and fair elections.

We can be shocked by the events in Minnesota. We can be disturbed by the lies and vitriol spewed by Trump’s supporters.

But we must remember that our first task is to prevent Trump from wielding even more destructive power. And that starts with protecting sensitive voting data — and the elections Trump wants to corrupt.
title: To restore the rule of law, Bondi wants us to hand over democracy

author: Marc Elias
body:
My conversation with Secretary Adrian Fontes30:38*Arizona Secretary of State Adrian Fontes isn’t new to the fight for democracy. Before becoming the chief election official in Arizona, he was overseeing elections as the Maricopa County Recorder. Fontes is one of our strongest soldiers for democracy, and he isn’t messing around when it comes to protecting free and fair elections. As he said, “it’s going to be a knock-down, drag-out fight.” And he certainly isn’t afraid of a fight.*

*This interview has been edited for length and clarity.*

**Marc Elias: Adrian Fontes, welcome back to Defending Democracy.**

Adrian Fontes: Happy to be here with you, Marc.

**Marc Elias: We are a year into the Trump presidency. We just recently experienced the one year anniversary of Jan. 6th. I want to get your take on the arc of time, whether it's the five years from Jan. 6th, whether it's the one year that Donald Trump has been in office, because what people may or may not know is you were in 2020 literally at ground zero for election denialism, for the circus that became the big lie.**

**The nature of the allegations in Arizona were always more preposterous, but it lingered in the culture much longer and some would argue is still lingering there today. So maybe you could start in 2020 and then move from then about what your reflections are.**

Adrian Fontes: Well, let me preface this by saying as much as I love Arizona — I'm a native, my family's been here for literally centuries — when it comes to politics, we put the AZ in crazy, especially since 2020. Now, as you said, I was the Maricopa County Recorder. The chief election officer for what became the second largest voting jurisdiction in the United States, the greater Phoenix area and all of those cities and towns. Almost two and a half million voters were in my charge for that 2020 election.

And don't forget, I was on the ballot for reelection as the Maricopa County Recorder, as was then-President Trump for reelection. Both of us lost. I didn't complain because I knew the system was solid and the folks operated with integrity. But right after election night, we started seeing those crowds gathering outside that warehouse, the QAnon Shaman, Jacob Chansley, Alex Jones, Congressman Gosar, chanting and screaming and yelling with absolutely no reason to do so. It was a security circumstance.

And eventually what ended up happening in Arizona, the state Senate ended up creating this Cyber Ninja audit weirdness and ballots were turned over to some absolute incompetence. Even former Secretary of State Ken Bennett, a Republican who was there to kind of watch things happen, said that it was just nonsensical. That was allowed by former president of the state Senate here, Karen Fann, which embarrassed the hell out of Arizona. Through all of that nonsense that was fueled by the conspiracy theories, the grift was created and the grift continued in the next several years.

Election denialism turned into Kari Lake running for governor and then Kari Lake running for Senate and all of these other folks running for offices, some of whom are now serving not just in Arizona but across the country.

We've got some of the bad guys inside of the castle now in election administration offices across the United States of America. And we're fighting a different kind of battle. Back then it was from the outside. Back then it was people who had been pushed out and they were banging on the door.

Now we've got them inside of the system and we've got to find the best ways to work through all of that to move forward so that we can continue with this democracy that we love and some of us signed up to volunteer to serve for. I spent four years in the Marine Corps and I never thought I'd see a time when members of our own government at the federal, state, and even local level now were working against our democracy.

So these last several years have been a hell of an adventure in American politics and I look back to just after that election day on Jan. 6th with kind of disdain, embarrassment. It was, I think, the worst political stain against this country that we have seen with the degradation of our capital, the violence, the unnecessary deaths, particularly of those folks who were trying to keep the capital safe. This is not a happy day. It's a day like I've said that'll live in infamy just like that other infamous day that we saw back in 1941.

**Marc Elias: My recollections of Arizona in 2020 was that it was a very well run election and not actually in the end all that close. And the first time I realized that the allegations being made in Arizona were just completely frivolous was a series of lawsuits that got dubbed "Sharpiegate." And this was a false claim that somehow Sharpies had marked ballots and had bled through. There was literally nothing to it. That isn't how the ballots work. But yet there wound up being this successive series of lawsuits on this.**

Adrian Fontes: It's funny because Sharpiegate is one of the coolest weird stories of my personal experience. I ran the 2018 election and the 2020 election. But the tabulators where the ballots went through were different systems from the same company. In the 2018 system, it was an optical scan. So all of the selections on the ballot front and back lined up because the optical scan thing was mechanical, it was like a Scantron. So they had to line up to the scanner. And if you used Sharpies in 2018, they could bleed through and that sometimes caused issues and we'd have to hand check those ballots. That was 2018.

In the 2020 system, we told people, "Please use Sharpies” because that new system, the tabulators, in front of the scanning lens had a plastic feeder that protected the lens. And if the ink didn't dry fast enough, that thing would get ink residue on it. We tested and tested and tested this. Well, Sharpiegate was based on this notion that there were old instructions out there that said "don't use Sharpies." And in 2020 we started saying "we do use Sharpies." And that became Sharpiegate because now we've got all these instructions and people weren't discerning 2018 instructions from 2020 instructions. The fact that the new stuff is a lot better, a lot faster, but needed a different kind of physical interaction between the voter and the ballot was the impetus for the whole thing. And we did not get the resources necessary to communicate to folks what the change was. The face that some people don't like funding the government or educating people means that this sort of thing could arise.

Like you've mentioned, it turned into a giant lawsuit and it turned into a giant scandal literally about nothing. And even some of those folks who filed these cases and filed these statements were sanctioned by the Supreme Court and disciplined by the state bar of Arizona. In fact, the guy I'm running against in November of 2026 is one of those "Kraken" lawyers of the Sidney Powell team.

**Marc Elias: Wow, I didn't know that!**

Adrian Fontes: Yeah, he's endorsed by Turning Point. But he has been sanctioned by the Supreme Court, disciplined by the state bar for lying in these pleadings that you probably fought directly against. We're still facing these challenges, but now these folks are in government. He is now in the State House of Representatives and he's going to be running against me for Secretary of State in my reelection this November. So we're still fighting these same fights against these same people. And it is their capacity to hornswoggle and gaslight folks that keeps them in the grift. And that's really unfortunate and sad that they have so little honor and such a lack of integrity. It just kind of bums me out.

**Marc Elias: You mentioned something which was actually one of the reasons why I wanted to have you on, which is about funding of elections. You said that you can basically have two of three things. Right?**

Adrian Fontes: You can do two or three. You can do accurate elections, fast election results, or cheap elections. You cannot have all three. If you want speed and you want accuracy, you've got to pay for it. It costs a lot of money. Now one of the other things I think that's important here is this notion of faster results. And there's a lot of people getting gaslit on this thing.

Network predictions are the only results you see on election night. There is no jurisdiction in the United States of America that gives you actual results on election night. That is a lie. What you see on election night is a prediction from the networks. They do their math. They've got their statisticians and all that stuff. Whether they're dependable or not is up to you all. It doesn't matter. The point is results take time and they take a lot of money.

Accurate results take more money. So: fast, accurate, or cheap? You can't have all three. You've got to pick two. We think, and we have always thought in Arizona, we need accuracy. That's always been number one, and that's what we've worked on. And the fact that Arizona likes doing things, my predecessor in the Recorder's office would say, "as cheap as humanly possible," means that we didn't have fast results. That's why Maricopa County always took a little time, but we always got it really, really right. We always got it really, really accurate, and we still do. So this is one thing I think people need to realize.

Election administration is its own monster. It's hugely complicated, but we make it seem very simple. I'll give you a great example. Just about everybody's got one of these [smartphones]. But most folks don’t actually know how to put it together. You and me can swipe, talk to somebody on the other side of the planet. We don't know how it all works. That's how election administration is, particularly in big jurisdictions. It's complicated. But we make it easy for the voters on purpose because we want Americans to have access. We want as many American voices involved in that little-d democratic decision-making process so that we can get the true voice of our democracy out there, the true power of the people reflected in the results.

That's what those of us who are fighting for every voter, regardless of party, regardless of affiliation... we want as much participation as possible so we try to make it as easy as possible while maintaining accuracy, security and integrity in these systems.

**Marc Elias: Before I was known as a general voting rights litigator, I was probably best known as a post-election litigator. I had done a series of recounts and election contests going all the way back to Mary Landrieu's 1996 Senate race. In 1998, I worked for Harry Reid in a very close election contest. In 2000, I was in Washington State for Maria Cantwell. Al Franken in 2008.**

**It’s interesting because in all of those instances, we prized accuracy. We didn't necessarily focus on speed. The fact is I didn't get to Minnesota, for example, for the Franken recount for four or five days after election night. I was just waiting for the full results to come in. And that's the same with Florida in 2018 when I went there for Bill Nelson after he narrowly lost his Senate race by a tenth of a percent.**

**Historically, there was never a reason to worry about this the day, the night of, or the next morning or even the morning after that. Because as you say, it took a while for the votes to be counted. But something happened in 2020 where the Republicans weaponized that process. And rather than treating that as getting it right, they treated it with suspicion. You would think that if they were really worried about fraud, you'd worry if the results came in too quickly, right? You'd say, "Well, how could that possibly be that all of these precincts and all of these provisional ballots and all of these mail-in ballots... how could it all be resolved on election night?" But they wound up flipping that on its head, Secretary.**

Adrian Fontes: 2018 was really interesting because I remember it vividly. It was Kyrsten Sinema and Martha McSally and the lead flip-flopped about every day for a week, right? And we got it right at the end of the day. And it was Maricopa County that everybody was looking at. That was my first big general election as an election administrator. And I look back at that and I think we got it right. That's what mattered. It wasn't about speed.

But here's the thing, when you want to destroy a system, you find every possible way to attack that system. The notion of attacking American elections because they're not fast enough is a red herring. It's not a real issue, but it's been turned into a real issue. It's turned into a real concern by misnaming network predictions as election results. They've been very successful at that. By saying that, as you've indicated, slower results means less dependability, which is exactly the opposite of what's true.

And so what they've done is they've weaponized the notion of accuracy by turning this little red herring thing into a problem and then turning it up on its head to say "this is why this is a bad thing." When you don't want to have something happen, you point out all of the reasons why it's bad.

They don't want elections to happen in this country. They're tired of democracy. They're tired of the fact that you as a voter get to decide who is in power, because every once in a while, you'll pick somebody other than them. They're tired of the fact that Americans get a choice and that Americans all get a chance to actually speak up.

That is the biggest problem that we've got and that we're fighting against. We want to hear every American voice even if they disagree with us. They don't want to hear any American voices except one. And that is anti-American. That's not un-American. It's anti-American. And the notion of weaponizing fast results as a problem in elections is another one of the pieces of their puzzle that they're trying to put together to discontinue the practice of free and fair elections here in the United States.

**Marc Elias: One of the tactics that Trump used in 2020 elsewhere was to try to prevent the certification of accurate results. And then in 2022, we saw in Cochise County, Arizona, an effort by the local election board to literally try to reject the results of a free and fair election and try to refuse to certify the results. If you want to talk about something that should be setting off alarm bells for anyone who cares about democracy, it's that, right? Tell us about that and what steps you've taken, what steps have been taken to try to prevent that in the future.**

Adrian Fontes: What ended up happening down there was the Board of Supervisors, which is like a county board of commissioners in many other states. Our Board of Supervisors acts as the county election board and they do what's called a canvas. That's the formal certification after tabulation is done. And then they push all of the results for state races and federal races up to the Secretary of State's office for that final statewide canvas. Cochise County said, "We're not going to do that."

And it ended up becoming something that was litigated. You were part of that litigation, as I recall. And one of those members of that board of supervisors is set for a criminal trial for interfering with those government processes. It hasn't happened yet, but they are still working on that. They were sued. They were holding the election director under scrutiny and she stood up to them. I happen to have her as the state election director now because she has integrity and she is a Republican. A Republican who believes in the rule of law and free and fair elections. And I'm a Democrat. I like people who stand up for what's right and for voters.

So once that fight started happening there, we set forth a series of rule proposals in our elections procedures manual. We just published the 2025 version certified by the governor and the attorney general as law requires. And basically what we're saying is: Look, we're going to go forward no matter what. You can have your weirdness down at the county level, but we're going to figure out the right way to make this happen so that the voters' voices are heard regardless of who the voters vote for. Now, understand the politics here. Cochise County, Arizona is a very red county.

But that doesn't mean that their votes don't count or that their votes shouldn't count. They are Americans, they are Arizonans, they are voting legitimately, and we're going to hold the system to account so that those voices get heard. That's the critical component here.

The problem that you're talking about here and that we're addressing is that there are now a lot of folks across the country who've been elected into these types of positions who are going to be looking at something like that, I fear, for the November 2026 cycle. We are expecting that things aren't going to go the way of the president's party, I assume. And I think I'm looking at places all across the United States of America where this could be problematic. So you're saying it's a five alarm fire. I agree.

We need to pay very close attention to who's making the rules, who's going to be doing all of those certifications right now. In fact, we should have been paying attention for the last year and a half to make sure that we've got the right people in place, but not just that, the right processes and procedures so that we can push legit results — which is what we always get in this country — through the system to get to our certifications so that those voices can be heard. Again, regardless of who they vote for. The American people are the ones with the power. That power should be reflected in those legitimate results that we've always seen.

Let me wax poetic for one second here. If the Constitution could be boiled down into two words, in my view, it would be this: due process. Due process for the criminally accused, due process for litigants, due process for voters. Due process is the rule of law manifest. And this is so critically important for us to remember. No person regardless of their station or place should be above the law. And every voter's voice should also be heard and counted based on those rules that are previously set. So I'm passionate about this and I'm glad you're bringing this particular point to our attention. Folks should be paying attention to what's happening at the most local level of election administration. That's where the sausage gets made.

**Marc Elias: This is why I was thrilled when you ran for Secretary of State. It's why I endorsed you and I'm thrilled you won for Secretary of State. Because you have the experience of running elections on the ground. And that's not to criticize secretaries of state or governors who have not. But you know the real friction points that voters face. And one of the accomplishments that I want to talk to you about that I just think you deserve an enormous amount of credit for is Arizona has an election procedure manual.**

**Every state should have this requirement. It's a model for the country. And it is the nuts and bolts of how elections are conducted. And so you're a nuts and bolts guy. This manual before you took office had been sort of waylaid. You inherited an old manual but you updated it, you got it through, you improved it, you clarified it. So talk about that as a priority of yours and how that has improved the voting process and experience for people.**

Adrian Fontes: The elections procedures manual in Arizona is basically the rule book for local election administrators and for the secretary, anybody involved in the process. In that manual, what we do is we take the statutes, we take the case law, and we lay it out in best practice form. It standardizes all of the practices of election administration for the whole state. Now remember, Arizona has very diverse jurisdictions. So Maricopa County, for example, is the second largest voting jurisdiction in the country with about two and a half million voters. Greenlee County, Arizona has like 6,000 voters. So we've got to make something that's workable for the great diaspora of population, geography, language.

We've got huge populations here that speak the Navajo language, the Diné language. We've got languages like Apache, which are not written languages. They're just oral. So we've got to have processes and procedures for dealing with all of the different stuff that Arizona has to make sure that it's standardized. This manual by statute is required to be published or I should say promulgated by the secretary once every two years. And that is to update the statutes. And if there's been litigation, the rulings from the court can get updated in there. And just to kind of make everything easily workable for the local election administrators who are not usually lawyers.

Well, for many, many secretaries past, the much maligned process, the much fought over process has not resulted in a manual being published. And so when I took office, I made it my number one priority. I got an election director to help me out with that the first time around who was probably the best election lawyer in the state of Arizona. And she was just amazing. Helped put this thing together, brought everybody in, and we put it out in 205. We did the same process. We went through, we got all the counties involved and promulgated it again. Again, approved by the governor and the attorney general. This is the first time that Arizona has had this manual published two issues in a row as required in a long, long time. So I'm very proud of that achievement.

But the great thing about it for voters is you get a standard experience in the entire state. Regardless of the resource allocation, regardless of the language, the geography, the spaces, you get a standard experience in the state. And it's so much easier when we go to do the things like you do, to litigate at the end. Candidates know what to expect, political parties know what to expect, everybody knows what to expect, and that's really what we want as far as a voting experience is concerned. Now, truth be told, the manual isn't made for voters. It's made for election administrators. It's not generated for public consumption, but we make it easy to understand language. So if somebody comes in and wants to be a marshal at a polling place or they want to be an election judge or one of these sorts of jobs, they can go to that manual and they can read what it says in plain language what their job is supposed to be based on the law.

We're super proud of that. And I really appreciate you bringing it up because it's something that's really important and really necessary. That way we don't have individual county officials just interpreting statute their own way. And you might have one county attorney over here saying something and another county attorney over there saying something different. It takes away all of the uncertainty and it puts Arizonans on the same narrow path insofar as their election administration is concerned. So it's fair. And again, that's part of that due process that I've been talking about that's so critically important. We have to run a society that has predictable rules when you've got fair contests.

That's how it works for America. You can't have this willy-nilly sort of just like off the cuff or shooting from the hip type of processes. You've got to have the rules laid out and I'm super proud of the team that I've got now that has helped put this together for a couple of cycles. We've done it and I'm super glad that we have because it lays out the rules for '26 in a way that really benefits voters from their election administrators' perspective.

**Marc Elias: I want to ask you about something that is of great concern to me. I've described this as what will be the biggest story of the 2026 election cycle: the effort by the Department of Justice to access voter files in all 50 states, plus the District of Columbia. These are sensitive records. Typically the Department of Justice would have no need for this because, you know, in Arizona you run elections along with the counties, right?**

**The federal government doesn't have an administrator of national elections. So the fact that Pam Bondi and Harmeet Dhillon, the head of the Civil Rights Division, they want this data from all 50 states. How should people think about this? How worried should people be? And what is Arizona's stance on this?**

Adrian Fontes: Well, I'll tell you right now, and I'll say it publicly, they're going to have to put me in jail if they want this information and have somebody else give it to them because I'm not going to do it. First and foremost, the information that they're asking for isn't all public. Some of it is. As you mentioned, the voter rolls pretty much are all public record. You've got to have some accountability. Who lives in what houses, how many ballots go to those houses in the mail? Your political party affiliation in Arizona, that's on our voter rolls. Nothing secret about that and you shouldn't be ashamed of it. It is what it is.

So there is some of that information that's already publicly available. But the last four of your Social Security number, your date of birth, things of this nature that are what we consider personally identifying information is not available to the public. And we like to maintain security on that stuff because we don't want identity theft or identity fraud to be happening. And so to that end, the state of Arizona has statutes that forbid the release of this information. I like to follow state law, but not only that, the Privacy Act of 1974 that was passed after concerns emerging from Watergate scandals were people's personal identifying information was being used by the federal government. It's against the Privacy Act for me to send that information to anyone.

Then you have this whole dimension of why is it that the Department of Justice says that it wants this information. That's a whole second set of considerations. So the first consideration is this: It would be illegal of me to release the information to the Department of Justice as they have requested it. Period. They're asking me to break the law and I won't do it.

Secondly, their motive for doing it is bonkers. Here's the deal about voter lists. They're not static. And in a state like Arizona where you've got nearly five million voters, 4.7 million folks on those voter rolls, it's dynamic. How many tens of thousands of people go through online motor division, our motor vehicle division websites, which is where a lot of our voter registration stuff happens?

We're talking about literally tens of thousands of transactions a day. So taking a snapshot isn't going to give you anything. Moreover, what they're saying is, "We want to do this with it." And then they said, "No, do we want to do *that* with it?" And their shifting justification tells me, A, they're either lying, one or several of the different justifications is a lie because it's misrepresenting what they actually want to do with it. B, what they say they want to do with it — and this is the main justification — "we want to check for voter fraud or any of that sort of stuff." A snapshot of the voter rolls is not going to do that for you. So the way they want to use the data is wrong.

And then you look to what is the actual role of the Department of Justice in the Civil Rights Division insofar as voter rolls are concerned. Their role is to make sure that federal law is followed. That means "Hey Arizona, what does your process look like?" Not "What do your voter rolls look like? What's the data in your rolls?" That's not what they should be analyzing. That's not what the law gives them. The law gives them authority over: Are you following the processes required by law? That's different from individual data. So what they're doing is invading into space where they don't belong. It would be illegal of me to comply with their requests. I'm not going to make myself a criminal because the Department of Justice wants to go do some other bonkers thing.

Moreover, I don't trust them. I don't trust them. We know that the Social Security Administration data ended up on some unsecured server to the tune of like 300 million people's data. Just nobody knows for sure if it was secured or not. I don't trust them. Marc, if you were an Arizona voter, I'd say I'm protecting your data. I'm protecting my data, my family's data, my friends and Republicans' data as well, and everybody's data. Look, we live in a data world and we all know the dangers of having that stuff out there and the possible negative circumstances against it. Why in the world would I just turn that over to a bunch of amateurs and a bunch of folks whose motives I can't discern?

I can't trust. I am absolutely refusing. If we haven't been sued already, we're going to get sued. I think we're already part of that litigation that multiple states have been sued by the Department of Justice. And it's going to take a lot more than just a court order to get me to turn this stuff over. It's going to be a knock-down, drag-out fight. I will not turn this data over as long as I am the Secretary of State here in Arizona. It's just not going to happen. And I have warned our local election officials as well in writing: it is against the law for you to turn this data over to the federal government as well. So we're doing everything we can to protect our voters, their information, and the integrity of our election systems from this invasive and unjustified Department of Justice request.

[Watch the full interview here >>>](https://youtu.be/b0rCb--xaNA?si=z_Z57YuROydzS6qS)
title: My conversation with Secretary Adrian Fontes
